_short_description: Reproduces the high-ceiling rounded-room condition from Vartanian et al. (2015), shown to elicit stronger beauty judgements and approach-avoidance decisions.
_published_finding: Participants rated rooms with high ceilings (vs. low) and curvilinear contours (vs. rectilinear) as more beautiful and were more likely to approach them. The high-ceiling effect held across stimulus type. Effect sizes: η² ≈ 0.26 for ceiling-height main effect on beauty.
_caveats: Vartanian et al. used 200 photographs of real interior architecture; we are reproducing a parametric synthesis intended to evoke the same perceptual condition. Cross-validation against the original stimuli is recommended.

The published effect was on beauty and approach-avoidance ratings, not directly on awe. L36 (cognitive.awe) is the closer Sprint-2 latent; restorativeness (L17) and aesthetic_affect cluster (L21–L26) also load.
Cross-cultural caveat: Vartanian's sample was Western; awe responses to high-ceiling sacred space replicate broadly (Yaden et al., 2019) but the *meaning* of awe varies (Keltner & Haidt, 2003).

_citation.apa: Vartanian, O., Navarrete, G., Chatterjee, A., Fich, L. B., Leder, H., Modroño, C., Nadal, M., Rostrup, N., & Skov, M. (2015). Architectural design and the brain: Effects of ceiling height and perceived enclosure on beauty judgments and approach-avoidance decisions. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 41, 10–18.
